questions.question: An athletic 28 year old man presents with intermittent claudication affecting the right leg particularly when running or playing sports. Examination is normal with present lower limb pulses. The vascular team query popliteal artery entrapment syndrome and request further imaging to evaluate further and help plan surgical management. What would be the most appropriate test?
choices: AP and lateral right knee radiographs
questions.detail: Popliteal artery entrapment syndrome is caused by symptomatic compression or occlusion of the artery by adjacent structures, most commonly the medial head of gastrocnemius. MRI is the favored modality because it best demonstrates the underlying muscular anatomy and aids in surgical planning. While ultrasound and CT can show the artery, MRI provides superior soft tissue definition.

questions.question: A 49 year old dialysis patient is referred to the rheumatology clinic with back and joint pain involving bilateral shoulders and hips. Radiographs demonstrate erosions and subchondral cyst formation with preserved joint space. An MRI spine reveals a low T1 and T2 signal lesion at the inferior endplate of L3 enhancing following contrast, with a normal L3/4 disc signal. Similar low signal intra-articular nodules are seen in the right shoulder. Which finding would indicate the most appropriate diagnosis?
choices: Congo red staining of synovial fluid
Growth of Staphylococcus aureus on blood culture
Weakly positive birefringent crystals on polarised light microscopy
questions.detail: The description is of amyloid arthropathy, which is common in long-term dialysis patients. It characteristically presents with preserved joint space, erosions, and subchondral cysts. MRI typically shows low T1 and T2 signal intra-articular nodules. Amyloid protein is definitively identified by Congo red staining.

questions.question: A 60 year old female with a recent diagnosis of scleroderma presents with dysphagia to both solids and liquids. A barium swallow is performed. What is the most likely radiological finding?
choices: Improvement of the flow of barium following the ingestion of warm fluid
questions.detail: In scleroderma (systemic sclerosis), the distal two-thirds of the esophagus (smooth muscle portion) undergoes fibrosis, leading to a dilated, patulous appearance with absent or reduced peristalsis. The upper third (skeletal muscle) remains unaffected. A mediastinal air-fluid level may be seen due to poor emptying.

questions.question: A 23 year old cyclist has uncontrollable hypertension and increasing right flank pain 36 hours after a road traffic collision. Initial CT showed a right subcapsular renal haematoma. Current ultrasound shows an unobstructed right kidney with a substantial subcapsular collection and an arterial resistive index of 0.9. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Page kidney is caused by a subcapsular collection (usually a hematoma) compressing the renal parenchyma, which activates the renin-angiotensin system and leads to severe hypertension. Radiological signs include an elevated resistive index (>0.70) and a distorted renal contour from the extrinsic pressure.

questions.question: A 3 year old child is seen with a painless right upper quadrant mass which can be balloted. An ultrasound confirms a heterogenous right upper quadrant lesion with cystic and solid components measuring up to 10 cm centred on the right kidney. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Nephroblastoma (Wilms tumor) is the most common renal mass in childhood. It typically presents as a large, heterogeneous mass that displaces rather than encases adjacent structures (unlike neuroblastoma). It often has solid and cystic components and can contain calcification.

questions.question: A 35 year old man was assaulted several months previously. He now presents with reduced visual acuity in his left eye and pulsatile exophthalmos. An MRI of the orbits is performed. What is the most likely finding?
questions.detail: Pulsatile exophthalmos following head trauma is highly suggestive of a caroticocavernous fistula. MRI hallmarks include a dilated superior ophthalmic vein with a flow void, enlargement of the cavernous sinus, and thickened/edematous extraocular muscles.

questions.question: A 55 year old man with HIV (CD4 154) has perihilar and peribronchovascular ill-defined nodular opacities ('flame-shaped') with surrounding ground glass and bilateral enhancing hilar lymphadenopathy. A gallium scan shows the thoracic abnormalities are NOT tracer avid. Which diagnosis is most likely?
questions.detail: The flame-shaped nodules and enhancing nodes in an AIDS patient are characteristic of Kaposi sarcoma. The negative gallium scan is a key differentiator, as lymphoma (the main differential) is typically gallium-avid.

questions.question: Hand radiographs of a 55 year old female show distal phalangeal cortical irregularity and joint space widening with marginal erosions at the DIP and PIP joints. There is adjacent soft tissue swelling and periosteal reaction along the middle phalanges. Bone density is preserved. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Psoriatic arthritis characteristically involves the distal joints (DIPs), shows marginal erosions, and is associated with periostitis and widened joint spaces with preserved bone density. Rheumatoid arthritis typically spares the DIPs and shows periarticular osteopenia.

questions.question: A 28 year old female with Crohn’s disease has an MRI abdomen. Which MRI sequence is most helpful in identifying fistulae?
questions.detail: While multiple sequences are used, fistulae are best identified on fat-suppressed T2-weighted imaging, where they appear as high-signal tracts. Post-contrast fat-suppressed T1 imaging is also useful to show enhancement of active tracts.

questions.question: An MRI of a 44 year old paraplegic patient shows a 50-mm mass adjacent to the right femur. It has peripheral low signal on T1/T2, central muscle-isointense T1 signal, and surrounding STIR hyperintensity. Which test is most likely to confirm benignity?
choices: CT angiogram of the lower limb
questions.detail: The features are typical for myositis ossificans (heterotopic bone formation), which is common in paraplegic patients. A radiograph showing mature peripheral calcification would confirm benignity. This is a 'don't touch' lesion; biopsy can be misleading and mimic osteosarcoma.

questions.question: A neonate with dyspnoea has a large lower zone opacity on chest radiograph. An MRI confirms a congenital diaphragmatic hernia. Where is the diaphragmatic defect most likely to be?
questions.detail: Bochdalek hernias are the most common variety (90%) and are classically located in a left posterolateral position. They are often large and can cause pulmonary hypoplasia. (Mnemonic: BochdaLek = Left; MoRgagni = Right).

questions.question: A postpartum woman with resolved pre-eclampsia is neurologically inappropriate. Clinically the differential is between PRES and a posterior circulation infarct. Which finding is most consistent with an acute PCA territory infarct rather than PRES?
questions.detail: Both PRES and acute infarct show T2 hyperintensity and occipital distribution. However, restricted diffusion is a hallmark of cytotoxic edema in acute infarct and is typically NOT seen in the vasogenic edema of PRES.

questions.question: A 55 year old male of average weight is undergoing a lung biopsy. 10 mL of 1% lidocaine has been injected, but the patient requires more. What is the maximum additional volume of 1% lidocaine you can safely administer?
questions.detail: The standard maximum dose for lidocaine is 3 mg/kg, which for a typical adult is approximately 200 mg (20 mL of 1% lidocaine). Having already given 10 mL, an additional 10 mL is the safe limit.

questions.question: A 10 year old boy’s spinal radiograph shows posterior vertebral body scalloping, anteroinferior vertebral beaking, and decreased interpedicular distance. Which condition is consistent with these findings?
questions.detail: The 'bullet-shaped' vertebrae with anteroinferior beaking, posterior scalloping, and narrowing of the interpedicular distance are classic features of achondroplasia. Morquio syndrome typically features a central anterior beak.

questions.question: MRI of a 3-cm hyperechoic splenic nodule shows low T1 and high T2 signal. Following contrast, there is peripheral enhancement on arterial phase and progressive central filling on delayed images. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: The centripetal enhancement pattern (peripheral to central) is characteristic of a haemangioma, which is the most common benign splenic tumor. Splenic lymphoma and sarcoidosis typically present as hypo-enhancing lesions.

questions.question: An MRI of an indeterminate adrenal lesion shows it is T1 hypointense and very T2 hyperintense ('light bulb sign'), with avid heterogeneous enhancement and NO signal loss on out-of-phase imaging. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: A: Hypointense T1, Hyperintense T2, Avid, Signal loss
B: Hypointense T1, Hyperintense T2, Minimal, Signal loss
C: Hyperintense T1, Hypointense T2, Avid, No signal loss
D: Hyperintense T1, Hyperintense T2, Minimal, No signal loss
E: Hypointense T1, Hyperintense T2, Avid, No signal loss
questions.detail: Phaeochromocytomas are classically T2-bright ('light bulb sign') and show avid, prolonged enhancement. Crucially, they do not contain intracellular lipid and thus do not show signal loss on out-of-phase MRI, unlike adrenal adenomas.

questions.question: A neonate is diagnosed with pyloric stenosis. Which ultrasound finding correlates with this diagnosis?
choices: Corkscrew appearance on upper GI contrast study
Muscle wall thickness of 2 mm on ultrasound
Pyloric canal length of 15 mm on ultrasound
Pyloric transverse diameter of 10 mm on ultrasound
Pyloric volume of 1.6 cm3 on ultrasound
questions.detail: A pyloric volume >1.5 cm³ is diagnostic. Other standard criteria include a single wall thickness >3 mm, pyloric length >15-17 mm, and a transverse diameter >11-13 mm.

questions.question: Regarding imaging in suspected pituitary apoplexy, which statement is most accurate?
choices: CT is sensitive for the diagnosis
High T1 signal helps differentiate it from other pituitary masses
The pituitary gland rarely demonstrates restricted diffusion on MRI
The pituitary gland usually shows peripheral enhancement
questions.detail: Pituitary apoplexy is often due to hemorrhagic infarction of a macroadenoma. Enhancement is typically peripheral (rim) around the central necrotic/infarcted area. The infarcted center often shows restricted diffusion on MRI.

questions.question: A 45 year old male has haematuria, proteinuria, and positive c-ANCA. What CT chest finding is most commonly seen in this condition?
choices: Increased mediastinal or hilar lymph node enlargement
questions.detail: The case describes granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA/Wegener's). Cavitating nodules or masses are the most characteristic thoracic imaging finding. Airway stenosis and pulmonary hemorrhage (ground glass) are also common.

questions.question: A 73 year old man has smooth periosteal reaction at the metadiaphyseal regions of the metacarpals and phalanges bilaterally on radiographs. Soft tissues are swollen but bones are otherwise normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.detail: This is Hypertrophic Osteoarthropathy (HPOA). The most common secondary cause is non-small cell lung cancer, making a chest radiograph the most critical screening test.

questions.question: A 55 year old female has SBO from intussusception. Laparotomy shows multiple small bowel polyps. She has dark spots on her fingers and a sister with cervical adenoma malignum. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome features hamartomatous polyps (which can cause intussusception), mucocutaneous pigmentation, and a family history. It is specifically associated with an increased risk of cervical adenoma malignum.

questions.question: A neonate with Turner syndrome has an anterior abdominal wall defect. Which features are associated with this defect (Omphalocele)?
choices: A: Midline, Absent covering, Liver herniated, Bowel complications
B: Midline, Present covering, Liver herniated, No bowel complications
C: Midline, Present covering, No liver, Bowel complications
D: Right paramedian, Absent covering, No liver, No bowel complications
E: Right paramedian, Present covering, Liver herniated, No bowel complications
questions.detail: Omphaloceles are midline defects covered by a peritoneal membrane, often containing the liver, and associated with chromosomal anomalies (like Turner). Gastroschisis is typically right paramedian, lacks a membrane, and is associated with bowel wall thickening/complications.

questions.question: A 62 year old woman has bowel obstruction, pneumobilia, and a calcified mass in the abdomen (Rigler's triad). What is the most likely site of obstruction?
questions.detail: In gallstone ileus, a large stone fistulates into the bowel (usually the duodenum) and most commonly impacts at the terminal ileum, which is the narrowest point of the small intestine.

questions.question: MRI of a middle ear mass shows high T1 and T2 signal with a peripheral low-signal rim and no diffusion restriction. Signal remains high on fat suppression. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Cholesterol granulomas are high signal on T1 and T2 due to cholesterol and hemorrhage. The peripheral low signal rim is from hemosiderin. Cholesteatomas would show restricted diffusion.

questions.question: A 28 year old female non-smoker has pneumothorax. CT shows multiple bilateral thin-walled cysts of uniform size distributed in all zones with normal intervening lung. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: The findings describe lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM), which occurs in ~40% of women with tuberous sclerosis. PLCH cysts are typically bizarrely shaped and favor the upper lobes in smokers.

questions.question: A 42 year old female has fever, malaise, arthritis, and tender red patches (erythema nodosum) on her legs. Radiograph shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which diagnosis is most appropriate?
questions.detail: Löfgren syndrome is an acute, usually self-limiting form of sarcoidosis consisting of the triad: hilar lymphadenopathy, erythema nodosum, and polyarthritis/fever.

questions.question: CT of a 49 year old woman shows colitis affecting the entire colon and rectum. Which feature is more suggestive of inflammatory bowel disease rather than pseudomembranous colitis?
questions.detail: Pseudomembranous colitis typically shows profound mucosal edema/thickening with hyperenhancement, but because the inflammation is purely mucosal, there is classically very little fat stranding. Significant pericolic fat stranding favors IBD.

questions.question: A 53 year old female has multiple bilateral endophytic hyperdense renal lesions, a splenic lesion, hilar nodes, and pulmonary nodules. What is the most likely primary malignancy?
questions.detail: Melanoma is a 'hypervascular' primary that characteristically causes hyperdense renal metastases and is the most common source of splenic metastatic deposits.

questions.question: When reviewing the position of an endotracheal tube in a neonate, which statement is correct?
choices: The tube tip should be projected approximately 1.5 cm distal to the carina
The tube tip should be projected approximately 1.5 cm proximal to the carina
The tube tip should be projected approximately 3 cm proximal to the carina
The tube tip should be projected around the level of T4
The tube tip should be projected at the level of the superior clavicular line
questions.detail: The ideal ETT position in a neonate is the mid-trachea, approximately 1.5 cm above the carina. The carina is typically at T3/4.

questions.question: A 10 year old boy has a large cystic posterior fossa mass with a small solid component that is isointense on T1, hyperintense on T2, and shows avid enhancement. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: A cystic lesion with an enhancing mural nodule in the cerebellum of a child is the classic appearance of a pilocytic astrocytoma.

questions.question: A 52 year old male has reduced cardiac ejection fraction, hand radiographs showing chondrocalcinosis and squared metacarpal heads, and cardiac MRI shows reduced myocardial signal. The liver is small and nodular. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: The 'bronze diabetes' clinical picture with heart failure, cirrhosis (nodular liver), and squared metacarpal heads with hook-like osteophytes is diagnostic of haemochromatosis. The reduced myocardial/hepatic MRI signal reflects iron deposition.

questions.question: MRI shows a T12 vertebral collapse that is hypointense on T1 and T2 compared to the disc. Which other feature correlates with malignancy over an osteoporotic fracture?
choices: Convex margin of the posterior vertebral body
Post-contrast enhancement in the vertebral body
Vacuum phenomenon of the intervertebral disc
questions.detail: Convexity of the posterior vertebral margin and extension of signal into the pedicles favor malignancy. Retropulsion and the vacuum phenomenon are characteristic of benign osteoporotic fractures.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding the management of hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC)?
choices: Transarterial chemoembolisation (TACE) is a curative treatment option
TACE and thermal ablation cannot be used together
TACE is indicated in Child-Pugh score C and D patients
TACE may be used for large, unresectable tumours
Thermal ablation is a curative treatment option
questions.detail: Liver transplant is the only truly curative treatment. TACE is a life-prolonging palliative treatment for large unresectable tumors in patients with preserved liver function (Child-Pugh A or B).

questions.question: A baby with esophageal atresia has a contrast study showing a short narrowing of the duodenum at D2 with normal distal bowel gas. What is the most likely cause?
questions.detail: Annular pancreas encircles D2 and is highly associated with esophageal atresia and Down syndrome. Duodenal atresia would show a complete 'double bubble' without distal gas.

questions.question: MRI of a 32 year old with parkinsonism shows T2 hyperintensity in the caudate, lateral thalami, putamina, and tegmentum, sparing the red nuclei and substantia nigra. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: The 'giant panda' sign in the midbrain from T2 hyperintensity is a classic MRI finding in Wilson disease. PKAN (Hallervorden-Spatz) would show the 'eye of the tiger' (low signal with central high signal) in the globus pallidi.

questions.question: A 6 year old has lobar consolidation with air bronchograms and no cavitation. What is the most likely causative organism?
questions.detail: Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcus) is the most common cause of lobar pneumonia in school-aged children and adults.

questions.question: A 42 year old has scoliosis, pectus excavatum, a prior spontaneous pneumothorax, and an aortic root diameter of 57 mm. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: The combination of connective tissue findings (scoliosis, pectus) and significant aortic root dilatation is classic for Marfan syndrome. Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of mortality in these patients.

questions.question: A 72 year old has well-defined lucent skull lesions without sclerosis and a 'blade of grass' lytic lesion extending along the cortex of the ulna. Bone scan shows diffuse uptake in L3 and peripheral uptake in the skull lesions. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: The 'blade of grass' sign and 'osteoporosis circumscripta' (lucent skull lesions) are features of the early lytic phase of Paget's disease. Bone scans show intense uptake due to active remodeling.

questions.question: Which imaging feature favors focal nodular hyperplasia (FNH) over hepatic adenoma?
choices: No uptake of hepatobiliary contrast agent on MRI
Central T2 hyperintense scar on MRI
Reduced tracer uptake on Tc99m sulphur colloid scan
questions.detail: FNH classically has a central scar that is T2-bright and shows delayed enhancement. Because FNH contains Kupffer cells, it shows uptake on sulfur colloid scans, unlike hepatic adenomas.

questions.question: A 35 year old has radiolucent filling defects in the renal pelvis, a flattened/sclerotic femoral head, and LUQ splenic calcification. What condition accounts for the findings?
questions.detail: The findings (splenic calcification, AVN of hip) indicate Sickle Cell Disease. Papillary necrosis is a common complication, appearing as radiolucent filling defects in the collecting system.

questions.question: A child’s trauma CT has features of 'hypoperfusion complex.' Which of the following is NOT consistent with hypoperfusion?
choices: Thickened jejunal wall with hyperdense mucosa
questions.detail: In the hypoperfusion complex (shock bowel), the adrenals and kidneys hyper-enhance, and the small bowel (especially jejunum) wall thickens with hyperdense mucosa. Conversely, the spleen and liver show reduced enhancement.

questions.question: A 53 year old with seizures and fever has MRI signal abnormality in the cingulate gyrus, insula cortices, and temporal lobes with small T1-bright punctate foci. Basal ganglia are normal. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: HSV-1 encephalitis classically affects the limbic system (temporal lobes, insula, cingulate gyrus) while sparing the basal ganglia. The T1-bright foci represent petechial hemorrhage.

questions.question: What is the most common finding in the thorax in rheumatoid arthritis?
questions.detail: While RA is associated with fibrosis and nodules, a small, often asymptomatic pleural effusion is the most common thoracic manifestation.

questions.question: A 40 year old female has dense subchondral sclerosis at the inferior aspects of the bilateral medial iliac bones with preserved joint spaces. What recommendation is most appropriate?
choices: MRI sacrum with T1, T2 and STIR sequences
Suggest clinical correlation and rheumatology referral
questions.detail: The description is characteristic of 'osteitis condensans ilii,' a benign, often incidental finding in multiparous women that requires no further workup.

questions.question: CT of a 71 year old woman with ascites shows scalloping of the hepatic and splenic contours by septated fluid (0–12HU) and calcified peritoneal foci. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Pseudomyxoma peritonei characteristically produces low-attenuation fluid that 'scallops' the surface of solid organs like the liver and spleen.

questions.question: Which of the following features on MRI spectroscopy/perfusion indicates tumour progression as opposed to pseudo progression?
questions.detail: Tumor recurrence shows an elevated Cho/NAA ratio and increased relative cerebral blood volume (rCBV). Radiation necrosis/pseudoprogression shows reduced rCBV and low Cho.

questions.question: A neonate born at term via elective C-section has respiratory distress at 5 hours. Radiograph shows normal lung volumes, perihilar airspace opacity, and fluid in the fissures. Symptoms resolve in 48 hours. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: TTN is common in term babies born via C-section (lack of labor 'squeeze'). The hallmark is fluid in the fissures and perihilar opacities that clear rapidly within 2-3 days.

questions.question: An MRI of a trauma patient 5 days post-op shows a right subdural collection with peripheral enhancement and restricted diffusion. Which finding is most important to prioritise?
choices: Bilateral regions of parenchymal SWI signal hypointensity
New collection adjacent to left frontal lobe
questions.detail: Restricted diffusion and peripheral enhancement in a post-surgical collection are diagnostic of a subdural empyema, a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate evacuation.

questions.question: A 46 year old non-smoker has predominantly lower zone emphysema and obstructive lung function. What other imaging would be important?
questions.detail: The patient has Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. This condition characteristically causes both lower-zone emphysema and liver cirrhosis; an abdominal ultrasound is needed to assess for liver disease.

questions.question: What MRI finding would confirm a diagnosis of sinus tarsi syndrome?
choices: Blooming artefact in the sinus tarsi on gradient echo
High STIR signal in the calcaneus
Low T1 and low T2 signal in the sinus tarsi
questions.detail: In sinus tarsi syndrome, the normal fat within the tunnel is replaced by low-signal scar tissue on both T1 and T2 sequences.

questions.question: CT of a 45 year old with RLQ pain shows a well-defined 3.5-cm area of stranding fat in the mesentery adjacent to the ascending colon. The colon itself is normal. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: The size (>3 cm) and location adjacent to the ascending colon favor omental infarction. Epiploic appendagitis is usually smaller and seen in the rectosigmoid region.

questions.question: Which initial staging imaging is most appropriate for locally advanced squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix?
questions.detail: Current guidelines recommend MRI pelvis for local staging and FDG-PET/CT for assessing nodal and distant metastatic disease in cervical cancer.

questions.question: A 15 month old has a largeRUQ mass inseparable from the liver containing multiple air-fluid levels and septated cysts. Alpha-fetoprotein is normal and the aorta is normal calibre. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Mesenchymal hamartomas are typically large, multiloculated cystic liver lesions in children <2 years old. Alpha-fetoprotein is normal, which helps rule out hepatoblastoma.

questions.question: A 30 year old male has a well-defined intraventricular mass arising from the septum pellucidum with multiple cysts, calcification, and mild heterogeneous enhancement. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Central neurocytomas are typically seen in young adults and are classic for their location on the septum pellucidum with a bubbly/cystic appearance.

questions.question: CT chest of a 21 year old shows an incidental well-defined, non-enhancing 11HU mass in the middle mediastinum just caudal to the carina. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Bronchogenic cysts are most commonly found in a subcarinal location and typically have fluid density and non-enhancing thin walls.

questions.question: A 27 year old male has sclerosis of the lunate bone and an ulna that is 5 mm shorter than the radius (negative ulnar variance). What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Kienböck disease is avascular necrosis of the lunate and is highly associated with negative ulnar variance.

questions.question: Which condition is NOT associated with increased liver attenuation (~100HU) on unenhanced CT?
questions.detail: Increased liver attenuation is seen with amiodarone, iron overload (thalassaemia), Wilson's, and Thorotrast. Hepatic amyloidosis typically causes reduced liver attenuation.

questions.question: A 45 year old has a 45-mm lucent lesion in the subtrochanteric left femur with a thin sclerotic margin and central calcified nidus. MRI shows high T1 signal. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: An intraosseous lipoma characteristically shows high T1 signal (fat density) and often contains a central calcified nidus.

questions.question: An 8 year old girl has rectal bleeding, arthralgia, a leg rash, and ultrasound shows intussusception and enlarged hyperechoic kidneys. What is the likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schönlein purpura) is a common pediatric systemic vasculitis characterized by the clinical triad of rash, arthralgia, and GI symptoms (bleeding/intussusception). It often affects the kidneys (nephritis).

questions.question: MRI shows a well-defined midline mass just deep to the posterior nasopharyngeal mucosa between the heads of the longus colli muscles. It is stable over 1 year. What is the diagnosis?
questions.detail: Tornwaldt cysts are benign midline nasopharyngeal mucosal cysts typically located between the longus colli muscles.

questions.question: A 23 year old female has a CT abdomen pelvis showing acute appendicitis. Additionally, there is an abnormality reported in the anterior mediastinum. Further investigation with a CT chest shows a well-defined lesion which is predominantly cystic and contains foci of fat and calcification. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: The description is characteristic of a teratoma, a germ cell tumor that commonly contains cystic areas, fat, and calcification [1, 2]. Thymomas and lymphoma are typically soft tissue density without fat [2]. Thymolipomas contain equal portions of thymic and adipose tissue but rarely calcify [3].

questions.question: A 7 year old male is under investigation for vitamin C deficiency (scurvy). Wrist radiographs show sclerotic epiphyseal margins, dense metaphyseal lines, and an exuberant periosteal reaction. What additional feature might be present on cross sectional imaging?
questions.detail: Scurvy leads to abnormal collagen development and a bleeding diathesis, frequently causing haemarthrosis [3]. Other features include the Wimberger sign (sclerotic epiphyseal margins) and Frankel lines [3]. Cloaca, involucrum, and sequestrum are features of osteomyelitis, not scurvy [3].

questions.question: A 76 year old man has obstructive jaundice and an ultrasound showing an echogenic mass at the biliary hilum. Right hepatic lobe bile ducts are dilated up to the second order ducts. According to the Bismuth classification, what would be the grade of the cholangiocarcinoma?
questions.detail: Bismuth Type IIIA involves the confluence and extends into the second-order ducts of the right hepatic lobe [4, 5]. Type IIIB involves the left second-order ducts, and Type IV involves second-order ducts bilaterally [5].

questions.question: A 25 year old female patient with breast implants presents with a painless lump. She is referred for 'triple assessment.' What does triple assessment involve?
choices: Examination, ultrasound guided biopsy, MDT discussion
questions.detail: Triple assessment is the standard of care for a breast lump and consists of clinical examination, imaging (ultrasound and/or mammography), and pathology (FNAC or core biopsy) [6].

questions.question: A 6 month old boy has hydrocephalus. Which statement regarding congenital aqueduct stenosis is most accurate?
choices: The posterior fossa has normal appearances
The third, lateral and fourth ventricles are dilated
Presence of a T2 flow void at the aqueductal level is highly suspicious
Ultrasound is not useful in the diagnosis of the condition
Post contrast MRI sequences are most helpful
questions.detail: The key feature differentiating aqueductal stenosis from Chiari or Dandy-Walker malformations is a normal-appearing posterior fossa [7]. Hydrocephalus affects the third and lateral ventricles, but the fourth ventricle remains normal in size [7].

questions.question: A 25 year old female with polycystic kidney disease has sudden onset severe headache. Unenhanced CT shows subarachnoid blood in the interhemispheric fissure. What is the most likely site of the intracranial aneurysm?
questions.detail: Polycystic kidney disease is strongly associated with intracranial berry aneurysms [8]. The anterior communicating artery is the most common site for these aneurysms, and rupture often leads to blood in the anterior interhemispheric fissure [8].

questions.question: A 56 year old female smoker with Sjögren syndrome has CT showing diffuse ground glass opacification. Which interstitial lung disease is most commonly associated with Sjögren syndrome?
questions.detail: While Sjögren syndrome is uniquely associated with the rare Lymphocytic Interstitial Pneumonitis (LIP), the most common interstitial lung disease seen in these patients is Non-Specific Interstitial Pneumonia (NSIP) [9, 10].

questions.question: A 3 year old girl has multilocular, expansile, cystic osseous lesions in the facial bones. The working diagnosis is cherubism. Which of the following is most accurate?
choices: The changes are histologically indistinguishable from ameloblastoma
questions.detail: Cherubism is an autosomal dominant condition that always involves the mandible [5, 11]. It presents with painless progressive swelling of the cheeks and is histologically similar to giant cell granuloma [11].

questions.question: MRI of a 38 year old woman shows a multiloculated cystic mass between the sacrum and rectum. It is high T2 and intermediate T1 signal with minimal wall enhancement. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Tailgut cysts occur in the presacral space and are frequently multilocular [12]. Anterior sacral meningoceles are usually associated with sacral bone defects, which are absent here [12]. Duplication cysts are usually unilocular [12].

questions.question: A 53 year old man develops anaphylaxis immediately following a contrast-enhanced CT. Following the ABCDE approach, what is the most appropriate next step?
choices: High flow oxygen via a non rebreathe mask
1 mg of 1:10,0000 adrenaline via intravenous injection
10 mg slow intravenous injection of chlorphenamine
questions.detail: According to the Resuscitation Council UK, the first-line treatment for an adult with anaphylaxis is 500 µg of 1:1000 intramuscular adrenaline [13].

questions.question: A 55 year old man with recurrent pancreatitis has MRCP showing a 7-mm main pancreatic duct and an enhancing solid nodule in the pancreatic head. No calcification is seen. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: Branch duct intraductal papillary mucinous papillary neoplasm
Main duct intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm
questions.detail: Main duct IPMN is characterized by main pancreatic duct dilatation (>3 mm) and atrophy of the gland [14]. The presence of an enhancing solid nodule is highly suspicious for malignancy within the IPMN [14].

questions.question: A 36 year old woman with Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) has an MRI brain and spine. Which of the following findings would NOT be expected in her condition?
choices: Expanded spinal dura causing posterior vertebral scalloping
Multiple, bilateral cerebral white matter high T2/FLAIR signal lesions
questions.detail: Bilateral acoustic neuromas (CP angle masses) are the hallmark of NF2, not NF1 [15]. NF1 is associated with optic gliomas, dural ectasia, kyphoscoliosis, and Focal Areas of Signal Intensity (FASIs) [15].

questions.question: A 25 year old asymptomatic male's chest radiograph shows increased transradiancy of the right hemithorax with a small hilum and reduced vascular markings. What is the most likely aetiology?
questions.detail: MacLeod (Swyer-James) syndrome is an incidental finding resulting from childhood bronchiolitis, leading to a small, hyperlucent lung with a small hilum and air trapping [16]. Poland syndrome involves pectoralis muscle absence but does not affect the lung hilum or vascularity [16].

questions.question: A 50 year old male has a lucent, expansile lesion in a rib. Scrutiny of the distal clavicle shows subperiosteal bone resorption. Following treatment, the rib lesion resolves. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Brown tumours are associated with hyperparathyroidism. Subperiosteal bone resorption (especially of the distal clavicle) is pathognomonic for hyperparathyroidism, and Brown tumours can resolve or become sclerotic after the underlying hormone imbalance is treated [17].

questions.question: A 31 year old male with pruritis has MRCP showing irregular 'string of beads' stricturing of both intra- and extra-hepatic ducts. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) classically presents in young males (often with Ulcerative Colitis) and shows a 'string of beads' appearance on MRCP [18, 19]. Primary biliary cirrhosis involves only the intra-hepatic ducts and primarily affects females [20].

questions.question: Trauma CT for a 50 year old male shows a nodular 70HU hyperdensity in the right adrenal gland with peri-adrenal stranding. What has caused these appearances?
questions.detail: High-density (70HU) material in the adrenal gland following trauma is diagnostic of adrenal haemorrhage [20]. Haemorrhage is more common than laceration and often results from compression of the gland between the liver and spine [21].

questions.question: MRI for a 4 year old with developmental delay shows a thick band of grey matter parallels the cortex but is located deep to it. Diminished sulcation is also seen. Which condition correlates best?
questions.detail: Band heterotopia (double cortex syndrome) is a form of grey matter heterotopia almost exclusively seen in females, characterized by a band of grey matter deep to the true cortex [22].

questions.question: An 82 year old unkempt woman has ataxia and confusion. MRI shows bilateral T2 high signal and enhancement in the mammillary bodies and medial thalami. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: The classic triad of confusion, ataxia, and ophthalmoplegia in a patient at risk of thiamine deficiency (self-neglect/alcoholism) defines Wernicke encephalopathy [23]. MRI hallmarks include T2 high signal and enhancement of the mammillary bodies [23].

questions.question: Which parameter is most powerful in predicting invasive adenocarcinoma over adenocarcinoma in situ in a mixed ground glass nodule?
questions.detail: Recent studies indicate that nodule mass (calculated from volume and internal attenuation) is the most powerful predictor of invasive adenocarcinoma in mixed ground glass lesions [24].

questions.question: A 27 year old male has a pedunculated bony outgrowth from the humerus containing cortex and medulla. Which of the following is most likely to be true?
choices: The bony protrusion is directed away from the joint
The humerus is most commonly affected
The cartilage cap is 2.2 cm in thickness
There are likely to be multiple osteochondromas
The stem is prone to fracture
questions.detail: Osteochondromas (bone exostoses) are classically directed away from the adjacent joint [25]. A cartilage cap thickness >2 cm in an adult is concerning for malignant transformation to chondrosarcoma [26].

questions.question: A 55 year old diabetic has an echogenic liver on ultrasound with poor visualisation of deep structures. Which of the following features is most likely?
choices: Liver density of 10HU more than the spleen on unenhanced CT
Increased tracer uptake on Tc99m sulphur colloid scan
Liver reflectivity less than that of the renal cortex on ultrasound
Hypoenhancement of the liver relative to the spleen on contrast CT
Signal dropout of the liver on out-of-phase MRI sequence
questions.detail: In fatty liver disease, the accumulation of intracellular lipid causes a characteristic loss of signal on out-of-phase MRI sequences compared to in-phase sequences [27]. On unenhanced CT, the liver would be at least 10HU *less* dense than the spleen [27].

questions.question: Which feature is more typically associated with congenital rubella infection than with congenital cytomegalovirus (CMV)?
choices: Ultrasound showing hyperechoic foci in a periventricular distribution
questions.detail: While there is significant overlap among congenital (TORCH) infections, rubella is frequently associated with cardiac anomalies, whereas CMV is not [28]. CMV calcification is classically periventricular [28].

questions.question: A 47 year old with multiple sclerosis has a new temporal lobe lesion. It has a complete rim of enhancement, surrounding mass effect, and markedly increased relative cerebral blood flow (rCBF). What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: The combination of a complete enhancing rim and markedly increased rCBF is characteristic of glioblastoma [29, 30]. Tumefactive MS typically has an incomplete 'open ring' of enhancement and reduced rCBF [30].

questions.question: An 18 month old girl has a calcified retrolental mass in the globe. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: A calcified intraocular mass in a child under 3 is retinoblastoma until proven otherwise [31, 32]. Retrolental fibroplasia (retinopathy of prematurity) usually affects both eyes and results in small globes [32].

questions.question: A 22 year old woman with a tunnelled CVC has patchy consolidation, 'finger-in-glove' perihilar opacities, and upper lobe volume loss. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis (ABPA) causes bronchiectasis with mucous plugging ('finger-in-glove'). It is highly associated with asthma and cystic fibrosis (suggested here by the upper lobe fibrosis and long-term CVC) [33].

questions.question: Which of the following periosteal features is most likely to be associated with a benign process?
questions.detail: Benign processes allow time for thick, dense periosteum to form. The 'soap bubble' appearance is considered a non-aggressive, benign sign [34, 35].

questions.question: An 18 month old boy has painless rectal bleeding. Meckel’s diverticulum is suspected. Which is the most appropriate test to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.detail: In the pediatric population, 95% of symptomatic Meckel's diverticula contain ectopic gastric mucosa, which is best detected via pertechnetate scintigraphy [36].

questions.question: MRI shows a uterine cavity bisected by soft tissue extending to the internal os. The external fundal contour is convex. Which Müllerian duct anomaly is present?
questions.detail: The external fundal contour is the key differentiator: a convex (or flat) fundus indicates a septate uterus, whereas a cleft fundus (>1 cm indentation) indicates a bicornuate uterus [35, 37].

questions.question: A 4 year old girl is diagnosed with Kawasaki disease. Which of the following radiological findings would be consistent with this diagnosis?
choices: Enlarged kidneys with loss of corticomedullary differentiation
questions.detail: Kawasaki disease is a vasculitis associated with coronary artery aneurysms and the characteristic finding of a thin-walled, dilated 'hydrops' of the gallbladder [38].

questions.question: A 25 year old with Cowden syndrome has a non-enhancing mass-like area in the cerebellum with a striated 'tigroid' appearance. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Lhermitte-Duclos disease (dysplastic cerebellar gangliocytoma) is a WHO grade I hamartoma that is pathognomonic for Cowden syndrome and has a classic striated MRI appearance [39].

questions.question: CT chest shows lower lobe honeycombing and a markedly patulous esophagus. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: The combination of a dilated (patulous) esophagus and basal lung fibrosis is diagnostic for scleroderma (systemic sclerosis) [40].

questions.question: A 67 year old male has a large lytic, expansile sacral lesion with internal calcification and T1 high signal foci. What is the most likely primary bone tumour?
questions.detail: Chordoma is the most common primary malignancy of the sacrum. High T1 signal foci representing hemorrhage are a relatively specific finding for this midline tumor [41, 42].

questions.question: Which of the following features favours a diagnosis of xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis over gallbladder carcinoma?
questions.detail: While both can look similar, the presence of multiple hypoattenuating nodules within a thickened gallbladder wall is highly suggestive of xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis [43, 44].

questions.question: Which sequence is most helpful in deciding if a cerebellopontine angle mass is an epidermoid cyst?
questions.detail: Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is the most helpful sequence because epidermoid cysts show restricted diffusion, whereas schwannomas and meningiomas do not [44].

questions.question: A neonate has truncus arteriosus. Which radiographic features are most likely?
choices: Enlarged heart, plethoric lungs, right-sided aortic arch
Enlarged heart, plethoric lungs, left-sided aortic arch
Enlarged heart, normal lungs, left-sided aortic arch
Enlarged heart, normal lungs, left-sided aortic arch
Normal heart, plethoric lungs, right-sided aortic arch
questions.detail: Truncus arteriosus typically presents with cardiomegaly and pulmonary plethora [45]. While 1/3 have a right-sided arch, 2/3 (the majority) have a left-sided arch [45].

questions.question: A 7 year old boy with a facial rash and seizures has Tuberous Sclerosis. Which imaging finding is most likely present?
choices: Focal areas of signal intensity in the deep white matter
questions.detail: Subependymal Giant Cell Astrocytomas (SEGAs) are a classic brain tumor associated with tuberous sclerosis [45, 46]. FASIs and optic gliomas are features of NF1 [46].

questions.question: Trauma radiograph shows a nasogastric tube tip projected over the left lower hemithorax and an obscured left hemidiaphragm. Which injury is suspected?
questions.detail: Deviation of an NG tube into the hemithorax is a classic sign of diaphragmatic rupture with gastric herniation [46, 47].

questions.question: A 4 year old with iron deficiency anaemia has skull and brain imaging. Which of the following findings is LEAST likely to be seen?
choices: Widening of the diploe and thinning of the inner and outer tables
questions.detail: In iron deficiency anaemia, the facial bones are usually spared [48]. 'Rodent facies' resulting from maxillary marrow overgrowth is instead a hallmark of thalassemia major [48].

questions.question: A 34 year old female has a thick-walled gallbladder and a 12-mm CBD dilated down to the level of the cystic duct, where a 3-mm stone is seen. What is the most accurate diagnosis?
questions.detail: Mirizzi syndrome is defined by extrinsic compression and obstruction of the common hepatic/bile duct by a stone impacted in the cystic duct or gallbladder neck [49].

questions.question: Which of the following types of breast calcification is most concerning for malignancy?
choices: Branching linear calcification extending towards the nipple
Calcification with a layered appearance, dependent on the MLO view
Coarse dystrophic calcification in right upper inner quadrant
questions.detail: Branching linear (fine pleomorphic) calcification in a ductal distribution is highly suspicious for DCIS [50, 51]. Layered 'tea-cupping' is benign [51].

questions.question: Neonate has a hazy left upper zone opacity followed by hyperlucency with mass effect and shift. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Congenital Lobar Overinflation (CLO) initially shows an opacity due to delayed fluid clearance, which then becomes a hyperlucent, overexpanded lobe with mass effect [51, 52].

questions.question: HIV patient has multifocal T2-high/T1-low white matter lesions in the parieto-occipital region without mass effect or enhancement. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: PML is caused by the JC virus in immunocompromised states and characteristically shows asymmetrical, parieto-occipital white matter disease without mass effect [53, 54].

questions.question: A 56 year old retired miner has CT showing tiny calcified nodules and 'eggshell' calcification of hilar nodes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Silicosis is a pneumoconiosis characterized by calcified nodules and classic 'eggshell' calcification of lymph nodes [55, 56].

questions.question: Stillborn has bowing of the femora and humeri with metaphyseal flaring, platyspondyly, and squared iliac wings. What type of dwarfism is this?
questions.detail: Rhizomelic dwarfism (e.g., Thanatophoric dysplasia) involves shortening of proximal segments like the humerus and femur [57].

questions.question: Where is a Meckel diverticulum most likely to be located?
choices: Antimesenteric border of the distal jejunum
Antimesenteric border of the distal ileum
Antimesenteric border of the proximal ileum
Mesenteric border of the distal ileum
Mesenteric border of the proximal jejunum
questions.detail: Meckel’s diverticula are classically located on the antimesenteric border of the distal ileum, within 2 feet of the IC valve [58, 59].

questions.question: A 15 year old female with short neck and low hairline has cervical spine fusion of C2/3. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Klippel-Feil syndrome is a congenital segmentation abnormality causing cervical fusion (often C2/3) and a characteristic clinical triad of short neck, low hairline, and restricted neck movement [60].

questions.question: Which radiological sign would be most indicative of an atrial septal defect (ASD)?
questions.detail: In ASD, the left-to-right shunt causes enlargement of the right atrium and right ventricle while the left-sided chambers remain normal in size [61].

questions.question: A 62 year old has a central brain mass that involves the corpus callosum and crosses the midline with ring enhancement. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Glioblastoma Multiforme (GBM) is the most common primary brain tumor to cross the corpus callosum. Lymphoma is a differential but usually shows more solid enhancement [62, 63].

questions.question: During common iliac artery stenting, the patient's blood pressure drops and they become tachycardic. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
choices: CT angiogram of the lower limbs
Deploy embolisation coils at the site of the stenosis
Re-inflate the balloon at the site of stenosis
Re-inflate the balloon proximal to the stenosis
Remove the vascular access sheath, wire and balloon
questions.detail: Sudden hemodynamic collapse during stenting suggests arterial rupture. The first step is to re-inflate the balloon *proximal* to the site to tamponade the bleed [63].

questions.question: A 12 year old male has Osgood-Schlatter disease. Which of the following is LEAST likely to be seen on the subsequent MRI?
choices: Bone marrow oedema on STIR sequence
Distension and fluid in the deep infrapatellar bursa
Oedema in the soft tissues and the Hoffa fat pad
Thickening of the distal patellar tendon
questions.detail: ACL tears are trauma-related and not a feature of Osgood-Schlatter, which involves repetitive microtrauma to the tibial tuberosity [64, 65].

questions.question: Which of the following is most accurate regarding retroperitoneal sarcomas?
choices: Distant metastases are a rare finding in leiomyosarcoma
The myxoid type subtype of liposarcoma typically shows low T2 signal
Liposarcoma are the most common retroperitoneal sarcoma
Calcification is rare in malignant fibrous histiocytoma.
Retroperitoneal angiosarcoma has rarely metastasised at presentation
questions.detail: Liposarcoma is the most frequently encountered retroperitoneal sarcoma. Leiomyosarcomas metastasize frequently to the liver and lung [65, 66].

questions.question: An outpatient 3 years after a living donor renal transplant has a 50-mm thin-walled, anechoic structure adjacent to the transplant. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Lymphoceles are the most common cause of a perirenal collection following transplant and can persist long-term [66, 67].

questions.question: Which feature favours a diagnosis of intralobar sequestration over extralobar sequestration?
choices: Systemic arterial supply from the aorta
questions.detail: Intralobar sequestration is enclosed in visceral pleura and drains via the pulmonary veins [68]. Extralobar sequestration has its own pleura and drains to systemic veins [69].

questions.question: A 40 year old woman with postural headaches has brain MRI showing midbrain sagging. What is the most likely pattern of meningeal enhancement?
questions.detail: Intracranial hypotension (CSF leak) classically presents with diffuse, smooth pachymeningeal (dural) enhancement [69, 70].

questions.question: A 36 year old male has bilateral hilar lymph node enlargement and nodular interlobular septal thickening. EBUS biopsy confirms a diagnosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: While imaging could fit Sarcoidosis, the question implies 'Lymphoma' as the given best answer choice, noting sarcoid staging but prioritizing lymphoma in this specific scenario [70, 71].

questions.question: Which elbow ossification centre is LEAST likely to be seen in a 7 year old child?
questions.detail: Using the CRITOL mnemonic, the External epicondyle is the last to appear, usually around age 11 [72, 73].

questions.question: A 22 year old female with eosinophilia has an ultrasound showing a 6-cm liver cyst with smaller adjacent daughter cysts. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: The 'daughter cyst' appearance and eosinophilia are classic for a hepatic hydatid cyst caused by Echinococcus [74].

questions.question: Which of these correctly describes a Chiari II malformation?
choices: Absent cerebellum and herniation of the occipital lobe through the foramen magnum
An occipital and/or high cervical encephalocele
Displacement of the medulla and cerebellar vermis through the foramen magnum
Peg-like cerebellar tonsils displaced into the upper cervical canal
questions.detail: Chiari II malformations involve displacement of the medulla, fourth ventricle, and cerebellar vermis through the foramen magnum and are associated with myelomeningoceles [75, 76].

questions.question: What CT finding would NOT be consistent with acute Budd-Chiari syndrome?
choices: Hyperenhancing early phase central liver enhancement
questions.detail: Caudate lobe hypertrophy is a hallmark of *chronic* Budd-Chiari; in the acute phase, the liver is more likely to be globally enlarged [76, 77].

questions.question: A 42 year old who lived in Asia has headache, seizures, and multiple parenchymal cysts at the grey-white junction with enhancement. A cyst is also in the fourth ventricle. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.detail: Neurocysticercosis classically presents with multiple small cysts at the grey-white junction and within the ventricles, often leading to hydrocephalus [77, 78].
